{friend} stares at a bridge of pure cloud floating above {room}.
Test the first step — push with your {body_part}, springy!
Skip across {count} cloud planks — they bounce!
A {adjective} mist swirls — wave through it.
Land on the far side with {friend} — high-five!
{sidekick} pins {count} clues on the board in {room}.
Connect them with {adjective} red yarn.
Hmmm — what's the missing piece?
Where does {sidekick} look for the final clue?
Tilt the painting carefully — TA-DA, the note tumbles out!
Wiggle the shoe — the clue pops out! It says 'TRICKED YA!'
{friend} clips the snorkel mask in {room}.
Slip into water — float face-down peacefully.
Spot {count} {adjective} parrotfish below!
Kick your fins — slow strong kicks.
Surface with a giant spout of imaginary water!
{quest_giver} stops at the canyon edge near {room}.
Shout a {adjective} word — listen for the echo!
Echo back {count} times — count each one.
Sing a long note — it ripples along the rocks.
{quest_giver} smiles — the canyon sings with you.
{trickster} unlocks the haunted kitchen in {room} — wooOOoo!
Pots float! Stir a {adjective} pot with a giant ladle.
Catch {count} flying spoons mid-air.
What does {trickster} cook up tonight?
Roll the dough — it wiggles by itself! Squish it down!
Slurp loudly — the noodles vanish before they hit your mouth!
{quest_giver} shows you a tiny silver key found in {room}.
Try {count} locked boxes — does it fit?
A {adjective} chest clicks open — peek inside!
Inside is a glittering map to a hidden garden.
{quest_giver} bows and unfurls the map. Adventure ahead!
{friend} steps out under the moonlit sky over {room}.
Walk softly — toe-to-heel for {count} steps.
Point at a {adjective} silver cloud and wave hi.
Hear a far-off owl — hoot back gently.
Sit on a bench with {friend} — breathe slow and deep.
{quest_giver} cranks open the nutcracker chest in {room}.
March in stiff-leg steps — {count} stomps!
Salute a {adjective} toy soldier marching past.
Twirl in a snowflake dance — soft and slow.
Bow to the sugar plum and step out of the dream.
{friend} hitches the wagon outside {room}.
Walk alongside, clicking your tongue — giddyup!
Cross {count} dusty hills with a {adjective} song.
Stop for water — share a cup with {friend}.
Roll into the frontier town — wave at the welcome crew!
{trickster} hops on a rocket sled at the top of the hill in {room}.
Light the {adjective} fuse — WHOOSH!
Steer with leans — {count} hard turns!
Halfway down — which prank does {trickster} pull?
WHEE! Down the hill in reverse — flip a thumbs-up to passing trees.
Streamers flutter behind you — the whole hill cheers!
{guide_mentor} touches the crystal wall of the tunnel under {room}.
Tap {count} crystals — each chimes a different note.
Walk slowly — every step glows {adjective} blue beneath your feet.
Reach the tunnel's end — a giant geode opens.
Step inside — sparkles swirl all around with {guide_mentor}.
{friend} hoists the flag on the ship outside {room}.
Pull the rope — heave-ho {count} times together!
Sing a {adjective} sea shanty in a deep voice.
Stomp the deck in rhythm — boom-boom-boom!
Sight land! Wave at the harbor with {friend}.
{friend} declares today Polka Dot Day in {room}!
Point at {count} dotted things around the room.
Spin in a {adjective} circle — your dots blur!
Tap your {body_part} on the floor in a dot rhythm.
End with a big dotted hug — squeeze!
{helper_townsperson} opens the apple orchard gate near {room}.
Stretch up and pick {count} ripe apples!
Polish each on your {body_part} — make 'em shine.
Toss a {adjective} apple gently into the basket.
Take one home and crunch with {helper_townsperson}!
{frenemy} struts onto the dusty street outside {room}.
Tip your {adjective} hat — squint into the sun.
Count down from {count} — fingers ready!
At zero — what do {frenemy} and you draw?
Spritz the air — both miss, both laugh, both wave the white flag.
Strum a {adjective} riff — a duel of music! Audience claps wildly.
{friend} drags the sled up the hill outside {room}.
Take a {adjective} deep breath — you can see it puff!
Hop on — push off — WHEEEE!
Steer with both {body_part}s — left, right, left!
Pile into the snowbank at the bottom — laugh and laugh!
{helper_townsperson} hands you a flyer — {needs_saving} is missing in {room}!
Call softly — 'here kitty, kitty' — {count} times.
Check {adjective} hiding spots: under tables, behind boxes.
Tiny mew — there! Behind the cushions!
Scoop {needs_saving} gently — {helper_townsperson} cheers!
{sidekick} powers up the drone squad in {room}.
Buzz {count} drones into the air — pretend propellers!
Fly a {adjective} formation — V shape with your arms!
What mission does {sidekick} call?
Click click click — capture every color! High five {sidekick}.
Whoosh down — drop the pretend parcel gently and salute.
{quest_giver} unrolls the galaxy map in {room}.
Hop to {count} planets — leap leap leap!
Wave at a {adjective} space whale drifting past.
Refuel at a comet — sip glowing fuel through a straw.
Return home — {quest_giver} declares the trip a triumph!
{guide_mentor} sets up a party in {room}. "Noble gases are the quiet kids at the party — they don't grab on, they just float and glow. Today our helper is Helium!"
{guide_mentor} hands you a Helium balloon. "Where does Helium want to go first?"
The balloon zooms up and bumps the ceiling. Reach your arms up high and wiggle your fingers like Helium dancing on the ceiling.
Helium hovers above the cupcakes but doesn't touch them — noble gases don't grab on. Tiptoe in a tiny circle, hands behind your back.
Helium drifts to a quiet corner and watches. Crouch down small and rest your chin on your knees like a sleepy balloon.
{guide_mentor} laughs. "That's what noble gases do — they show up but they don't stick to anyone."

templates.title: Neon lights up the sign shop

steps.text: {guide_mentor} owns a sign shop in {room}. "Noble gases glow different colors when electricity tickles them. Neon glows bright orange-red!"
What kind of sign should Neon light up tonight?
The sign blinks ICE CREAM in orange-red letters! Lick an imaginary cone slowly.
PIZZA flashes bright. Pretend to toss a big pizza dough into the air and catch it.
NOW SHOWING glows above a tiny theater. Make a movie clapper with your hands — clap once!
TOYS sparkles in red across the window. Wave at the toys inside as if greeting old friends.
{guide_mentor} flips the open sign. "Neon doesn't say much — but boy, does it shine."
{guide_mentor} pulls a giant pretend lightbulb out of a drawer. "Noble gases protect things by NOT reacting. Argon fills lightbulbs so they don't burn out."
The bulb is empty inside. Cup your hands around it gently — Argon is going to fill it up.
You whisper "hush, hush" and pretend Argon tiptoes inside. The bulb glows softly and stays cool forever.
You take a small breath and blow gently. Argon drifts inside without a sound. The bulb is safe.
{guide_mentor} flicks the switch. The bulb lights up steady and quiet — Argon is doing its job.
{guide_mentor} holds up a pretend camera. "Noble gases are quiet, but Xenon can flash SO bright it freezes a moment forever. Smile!"
What kind of photo do you want Xenon to flash?
Stick out your tongue and cross your eyes. FLASH! Xenon caught the goofiest picture in history.
Hands on hips, chin up, cape billowing (imaginary). FLASH! Xenon froze you mid-power-pose.
Stretch your arms up and yawn HUGE. FLASH! Xenon caught the biggest yawn ever taken.
{guide_mentor} pretends to hand you the photo. "Noble gases don't talk much — but Xenon sure knows how to make an entrance."

templates.title: Chlorine finds a friend named Sodium

steps.text: {guide_mentor} sprinkles imaginary salt on the table. "Halogens are friend-makers! Chlorine pairs up with Sodium to make every grain of salt you've ever eaten."
Chlorine is wandering around {room} looking for Sodium. How does it find a friend?
You wave both arms big. Sodium spots you across the kitchen and waves back!
You stretch one hand out. Sodium runs over and grabs it tight — now you're salt!
Your humming carries across the room. Sodium follows the tune and pops up beside you.
Snap your fingers together once — CLICK! That's what halogens do; they grab hold and make something new.
{guide_mentor} holds up a pretend toothbrush. "Halogens love joining teams. Fluorine teams up with toothpaste to make teeth strong and shiny!"
Fluorine needs to find the right teeth to help. Which tooth does Fluorine visit first?
Fluorine waves at the wiggly tooth — "Hang in there, little buddy!" Wiggle one finger like a loose tooth.
Fluorine high-fives the new molar way in the back. Open your mouth wide and tap the air with your finger.
Fluorine polishes the front tooth until it shines. Give a HUGE smile and bare your teeth like a friendly tiger.
{guide_mentor} cheers. "That's halogens for you — always joining the team that needs them."

templates.title: Iodine sneaks into the salt shaker

steps.text: {guide_mentor} unscrews a pretend salt shaker. "Halogens are quiet helpers. Iodine hides in your salt and helps your body grow strong."
Where does tiny Iodine want to ride along today?
Iodine hitches a ride on a salty pretzel. Pretend to take a big crunchy bite — CRUNCH!
Iodine sprinkles onto warm eggs. Pretend to scoop a forkful and blow on it before tasting.
Iodine dusts the popcorn. Toss imaginary popcorn up and catch it in your mouth — gotcha!
{guide_mentor} pats your head. "Halogens are friend-makers — even when you can't see them, they're helping."
{guide_mentor} tosses a pretend pool noodle. "Halogens keep things clean! Chlorine swims around in pools chasing germs away."
Pick your swim stroke — how does Chlorine zoom around the pool?
Pump your arms in circles, kick your feet small and quick — Chlorine sweeps the lane!
Lie back, sweep your arms one at a time over your head — slow and smooth. Chlorine looks up at the sky.
Paws up, splash splash — Chlorine paddles like a happy puppy after a tennis ball.
{guide_mentor} blows a lifeguard whistle. "Halogens are friend-makers and germ-chasers. The pool is sparkly clean — good job, Chlorine!"
{guide_mentor} holds up a pretend battery. "Alkali metals are go-getters — they wake everything up. Lithium powers phones, tablets, and toys!"
Which toy does Lithium want to bring to life?
VROOM! The racecar zips across the floor. Crouch low and shoot your hands forward like a racecar.
BEEP-BOP! The robot stomps to life. March in place with stiff robot arms.
WHIRR! The drone lifts off. Spin in a slow circle with your arms out like helicopter blades.
PLINK PLONK! The keyboard plays itself. Tap your fingers in the air like you're tickling the ivories.
{guide_mentor} bows. "That's what alkali metals do — they make things go."
{guide_mentor} peels an imaginary banana. "Alkali metals get things moving! Potassium lives inside bananas and helps your muscles run."
Stretch your legs first — what warm-up does Potassium do?
Bend down slowly and reach for your toes. Potassium loosens every muscle on the way.
Big arm circles forward, then backward. Potassium is humming in your shoulders now.
Hop three times on the right foot, three times on the left. Potassium fizzes through your legs.
{guide_mentor} waves a flag. "Alkali metals are go-getters — now go go go!" Run in place as fast as you can for five seconds.
{guide_mentor} points at a pretend streetlamp. "Alkali metals wake the world up. Sodium burns golden-yellow in old streetlights, lighting the way home."
Who needs Sodium's light tonight?
The streetlamp clicks on. The cat tiptoes through the golden glow. Tiptoe four steps and say "meow."
The bike whizzes through the warm yellow circle. Pedal your hands in fast circles like wheels.
The umbrella sparkles in the golden light. Open imaginary umbrella overhead and spin once.
{guide_mentor} smiles. "Sodium's job is done. Alkali metals are the wake-up alarms of the periodic table."
{guide_mentor} taps a pretend wristwatch. "Alkali metals can be SUPER precise. Cesium runs the most accurate clock in the world — it only loses one second every million years!"
How does Cesium count its perfect seconds?
Tap your right foot, then left, then right, then left. Cesium is counting every single tick perfectly.
Snap your fingers four times, slow and even. Each snap is another perfect Cesium second.
Nod your head left, then right, then left, then right. Cesium keeps the rhythm steady.
{guide_mentor} taps the clock face. "Alkali metals make things go — and sometimes they make them go EXACTLY right."
{guide_mentor} flexes a tiny muscle. "Alkaline earths are shy helpers — strong but quiet. Calcium builds your bones every day while you're not looking!"
What does Calcium snack on first?
Pretend to drink a glass of milk in three big gulps. Calcium rushes to your shin bones.
Nibble a cheese cube — squeak squeak. Calcium hops into your elbows.
Scoop yogurt slowly — yum. Calcium drifts into your finger bones, then your jaw.
{guide_mentor} stacks pretend blocks. "Alkaline earths don't shout, but they do the strong stuff. Stand up tall — that's Calcium holding you up."

templates.title: Magnesium and the old flash camera

steps.text: {guide_mentor} picks up a pretend antique camera. "Alkaline earths are quiet — but Magnesium can burn with a SUPER bright white flash. Old cameras used it for pictures!"
Who is Magnesium taking a picture of today?
Curl up small and rest your head on your hands. POP! Magnesium flashes — the puppy's eyes shine.
Hold up imaginary candles and puff your cheeks ready to blow. POP! Magnesium catches the wish.
Squat down low, then SPRING up. POP! Magnesium freezes the highest part of your jump.
{guide_mentor} holds up a pretend photo. "Alkaline earths are shy helpers — but every now and then, they put on a show."
{guide_mentor} points up at the imaginary sky. "Alkaline earths are quiet during the day — but at night, Strontium paints fireworks bright red!"
What shape does Strontium spray across the sky?
Draw a heart in the air with both pointer fingers. BOOM! Strontium fills it in red sparks.
Sweep your arm down like a shooting star. BOOM! Strontium trails red light behind it.
Spin your finger in a big spiral above your head. BOOM! Strontium swirls the sky red.
{guide_mentor} claps. "Alkaline earths are shy most of the time, but they sure can dress up for a holiday!"
{guide_mentor} ties on a pretend leather apron. "Transition metals are the shiny crafters — strong, shapeable, useful. Today we're working with Iron!"
The forge is glowing. What does Iron want to be shaped into?
BANG BANG BANG! Hammer your fist on your other palm three times. The horseshoe takes shape.
Twist your wrist slowly — Iron bends. Make a CREAK sound as the new hinge opens and closes.
Pat the air with flat hands, shaping a wide round pan. Pretend to flip an imaginary pancake — flip!
Cup your hands and pretend to swing a tiny bell — DING DING. Iron rings clear and bright.
{guide_mentor} dunks the piece in water — HISSSS. "Transition metals shape the everyday world. Nice work, blacksmith."
{guide_mentor} flips a pretend penny. "Transition metals travel! Copper has been a coin, a wire, a pipe, and a pot. Let's roll with it."
Where does Copper roll first?
Drop the penny in — CLINK! Cup your hands and shake them like a piggy bank full of coins.
Zip through the wire — BRRZZT! Wiggle your fingers fast like electricity zooming home.
Plop into the pot — STIR STIR STIR. Move your hand in a big slow circle, stirring imaginary soup.
{guide_mentor} polishes the penny on a sleeve. "That's a transition metal — shiny, useful, and always going somewhere new."
{guide_mentor} unrolls a pretend cloth. "Transition metals shine. Silver is the SHINIEST of them all — the king of mirrors and the queen of spoons."
What does Silver shine up today?
Rub your hand in a big slow circle as if buffing glass. The mirror catches your smile.
Pretend to polish a long spoon top to bottom, then peer at your face in it — upside down!
Cup the ornament gently and rub with a thumb. The light bounces around the room.
{guide_mentor} holds the piece up to a lamp. "Transition metals don't hide. Silver always wants to be seen."

templates.title: Gold is forged into a crown

steps.text: {guide_mentor} bows low. "Today we honor the royal of transition metals — Gold! Gold never rusts, never tarnishes, and is the softest of the shiny crafters."
Whose crown is Gold forging tonight?
Twist your fingers in the air — tall spiky points for the queen. Stand tall and stretch your chin up royally.
Bend Gold into a low, rough band with a skull on top. Squint one eye and growl like a sea captain.
Pinch Gold into tiny twirls — leaves and flowers around the rim. Tiptoe on your toes like a fairy in the meadow.
{guide_mentor} lifts the crown high. "Transition metals shape stories. Today, Gold made a brand-new one. Bow your head and be crowned!"
{guide_mentor} unrolls a blueprint. "Transition metals can be tough AND light. Titanium builds airplanes, ships, and even rockets — let's make a spaceship!"
Which part of the spaceship does Titanium make first?
Press your palms together in a pointy shape — that's the nose cone slicing the sky.
Stretch your arms wide and tilt left, then right — the wings are testing the wind.
Stomp twice — BOOM BOOM! The boosters are bolted on tight.
Make a circle with your hands and peek through it like a porthole into space.
{guide_mentor} counts down — 3, 2, 1, BLAST OFF! Crouch low and leap up. "Transition metals build the stuff that lasts."
{guide_mentor} holds up a pretend soda can. "Post-transition metals are the soft ones — bendy, foldable, friendly. Aluminum makes soda cans and kitchen foil!"
What does Aluminum want to become today?
Press flat palms to your head and shape an imaginary hat. Tip the brim and bow like a foil knight.
Crumple imaginary foil in two hands — squish, squish! Now toss it up and catch it.
Pull your hands apart slowly — Aluminum stretches into a long ribbon. Wave it side to side like a banner.
{guide_mentor} pats the foil flat. "Post-transition metals are gentle metals — strong enough to hold soup, soft enough for tiny hands."
{guide_mentor} lines up pretend tiny soldiers. "Post-transition metals are soft and friendly. Tin used to make toy soldiers and still coats the inside of food cans!"
What drill do the Tin soldiers run today?
March four steps forward, four right, four back, four left. Tin clinks at every step.
Spin in place once, then snap a salute to your forehead. Tin gleams under the lamp.
Tiptoe four slow steps, finger on your lips. The cat doesn't wake up. Whew.
{guide_mentor} blows a tiny pretend trumpet. "Post-transition metals don't shout — they march quietly and get the job done."
{guide_mentor} cradles a pretend crystal. "Post-transition metals can be sparkly too. Bismuth grows into rainbow staircase crystals that look like teeny castles!"
Which color stair does Bismuth grow first?
Step up — UP — UP three little steps. Each step glitters pink and purple.
Take three slow giant steps. Each step is glassy green and ocean blue.
Tap three times in place — each tap shines gold and orange like a tiny sunrise.
{guide_mentor} peeks inside. "Post-transition metals are the soft ones — but they sure can dress up like a rainbow."
{guide_mentor} squints into a tiny pretend microscope. "Metalloids are in-betweeners — not quite metal, not quite not. Silicon hides in beach sand AND every computer chip!"
What does Silicon build into the chip today?
Hold up fingers — one, two, three, four, five! Silicon's circuit can count higher than the stars.
Hum a quick tune — la la LA! Silicon's circuit just learned your song.
Wiggle all ten fingers in front of your face. Silicon's circuit lights up like a million tiny stars.
{guide_mentor} slides the chip into place. "That's a metalloid for you — small but mighty, the in-between hero of every screen."
{guide_mentor} digs into a pretend sandbox. "Metalloids are in-betweeners — half metal, half something else. Boron makes glass and ceramics extra strong!"
What does Boron help the sand castle become?
Pat your hands flat one on top of the other — the tower rises higher and higher. Boron keeps it from crumbling.
Sweep your hand in a wide ring around your imaginary castle. The water is deep. Boron keeps the walls firm.
Lay your forearm flat — that's the drawbridge. Lift it up, then lay it back down. Boron keeps it from cracking.
{guide_mentor} pats the castle gently. "In-betweeners hold things together. Boron is the secret ingredient."
{guide_mentor} dusts off a pretend old computer. "Metalloids started the computer age! Germanium was inside the very first tiny switches called transistors."
What should Germanium's computer do first?
Hold up three fingers, then four — push them together — that's seven! Germanium just did math.
Wave at the screen and say "hi there!" Germanium types the letters one by one.
Tap an imaginary key three times — BEEP BEEP BOOP. Germanium makes a tiny tune.
{guide_mentor} pats the old machine. "Metalloids are the in-betweeners that built the modern world. Thanks, Germanium."
{guide_mentor} takes a big slow breath. "Nonmetals are everywhere essentials — invisible but crucial. Oxygen rides into your lungs on every breath!"
Where does Oxygen want to ride next?

choices.label: Through a flame on a candle

steps.text: Flutter your hands fast like hummingbird wings. Oxygen keeps the wings buzzing!
Run in place faster, faster. Oxygen powers every step. Slow down and catch your breath.
Wave one finger like a flickering flame. Oxygen feeds the fire — but stays out of your reach.
{guide_mentor} bows. "Nonmetals are the everyday helpers. Take one big breath together — thanks, Oxygen!"
{guide_mentor} sketches a stick figure. "Nonmetals are everywhere essentials. Carbon is the building block of every living thing — trees, dogs, even you!"
Who does Carbon say hello to first?
Stand tall, stretch your arms out like branches, and sway gently. Carbon is in every leaf.
Tuck your head into your shoulders like a turtle in a shell. Carbon makes that shell strong.
Wiggle your whole body side to side like a happy garden worm. Carbon zooms through the dirt.
Crouch down small, then s-l-o-w-l-y stretch up like a sprout reaching for the sun. Carbon builds every leaf.
{guide_mentor} smiles wide. "Nonmetals quietly hold the world together. Carbon is in YOU too — tap your chest twice. Hi, Carbon!"
{guide_mentor} fans the air dramatically. "Nonmetals can be stinky too! Sulfur is the bright yellow stuff near volcanoes — and it smells like rotten eggs!"
Where does Sulfur stink up today?
Pretend to bubble your fingers up like rising bubbles. Pinch your nose — STINKY hot spring!
Pretend to crack an egg, then sniff and make a goofy face. Sulfur is the egg smell.
Strike an imaginary match — SCRATCH! Wave it gently. Sulfur is what makes the match light.
{guide_mentor} laughs and fans the room. "Even smelly nonmetals are everywhere essentials. Thanks, Sulfur — now go take a bath!"
{guide_mentor} sweeps an arm across the air. "Nonmetals are everywhere essentials — most of the air around you is Nitrogen, even more than Oxygen!"
What does Nitrogen want to do with all that air?
Pretend to hold a kite string and tug it back and forth. Nitrogen lifts the kite higher and higher.
Puff your cheeks and blow — WHOOSH! Nitrogen pushes the boat across the bay.
Push your hands flat down on imaginary dough, then watch it rise. Nitrogen makes the bubbles fluffy.
{guide_mentor} takes a slow deep breath. "Most of every breath is Nitrogen. Nonmetals are quiet — but they're EVERYWHERE."
{friend} sets their red car down to grab a snack. When they look back — the car is gone. Across the room, {frenemy} is holding it. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s shoulders go stiff. From here it looks like {frenemy} grabbed the car the second {friend} turned away. {friend} feels their chest squeeze.
{friend}'s eyebrows pull down. {frenemy} did not ask once. From here that looks like {frenemy} thinks {friend}'s things are also theirs.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} did something wrong. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was {frenemy} actually doing with the red car?
{frenemy} saw a wheel wobble loose as {friend} walked away. They picked it up to push the wheel back on before {friend} came back to a broken car.
{frenemy} saw the dog walking right toward the car. They scooped it up fast so the dog would not chew the wheel. They were going to bring it right back.
{friend} thought one thing. {frenemy} was doing another. Both had real reasons inside their head.
{friend} is in line for the slide. They step aside for one second to fix their shoe. When they look up — {frenemy} has climbed onto the ladder ahead of them. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s jaw goes tight. From here it looks like {frenemy} waited for {friend} to look down, then snuck past on purpose.
{friend}'s feet feel stuck. From here it looks like {frenemy} did not even notice {friend} was there. That feels worse than the skipping.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} cut in line. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What did {frenemy} see when they walked up to the ladder?
{frenemy} walked up and saw nobody on the ladder, nobody at the bottom. They thought {friend} had gone to the water fountain and given up their spot.
{frenemy} saw the kid in front move on, and nobody stepping up. They thought the line had ended and they were the next one to go.
{friend} saw a line skip. {frenemy} saw an empty ladder. Both were really there at the same time.
Two cookies sit on a plate at snack time. {friend} saves their cookie for later. When {friend} comes back, the plate is empty and {frenemy} is wiping crumbs off their chin. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s tummy drops. From here it looks like {frenemy} knew that second cookie belonged to {friend} and ate it anyway.
{friend}'s eyes sting. From here it looks like {frenemy} doubled up — one for them, one for them too. {friend} feels small.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} took what was theirs. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. When {frenemy} walked back to the plate, what did they think they were seeing?
{frenemy} remembered eating their cookie earlier. Coming back to the plate, they saw one cookie left and thought {friend} had already eaten theirs and this leftover was up for grabs.
{frenemy} got mixed up about which cookie was which. They saw the one closer to their chair and thought it was the one they had set aside for themself.
{friend} saw a stolen cookie. {frenemy} saw a leftover. Same plate, two stories.

templates.title: The kid who won't come over

steps.text: {friend} sets up a tea party. They wave at {frenemy} across the playroom. {frenemy} just sits there watching, not coming over. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s shoulders slump. From here it looks like {frenemy} could come over but is choosing not to. {friend} feels left alone with the teapot.
{friend}'s heart pinches. From here it looks like {frenemy} is staying away because they do not want to be friends today. That stings.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} is shutting them out. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was going on inside {frenemy}'s head as they watched the tea party?
{frenemy} thought the tea party was {friend}'s special game. They were waiting for {friend} to say 'come play' out loud, because a wave alone did not feel like enough of an invitation.
{frenemy} wanted to come over so badly. But their feet felt shy and stuck. They were hoping {friend} would walk halfway, so the last few steps would feel easier.
{friend} thought {frenemy} did not want to play. {frenemy} was actually waiting for a real invite.
{friend} is counting to twenty for hide-and-seek. At twenty, they look up — {frenemy} is hiding behind the couch, but {friend} can see one whole foot sticking out. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s mouth opens. From here it looks like {frenemy} picked a spot they KNEW had a peeking gap, to make the round end fast on purpose.
{friend}'s breath huffs out. From here it looks like {frenemy} did not even try to hide well. That feels like they did not want to play right.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} broke the game. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. Why did {frenemy} pick behind the couch?
From where {frenemy} crouched, the couch looked HUGE. They could not see their own foot. They thought behind-the-couch was a totally fair hiding place.
{frenemy} had played here once before and remembered the couch as their best spot. They tucked in fast and never thought to check what {friend} would see from the other side of the room.
{friend} thought it was cheating. {frenemy} thought it was just hiding. They were looking from two different angles.
{friend} draws their best-ever rainbow and slides it across the table to {frenemy}. {frenemy} looks at it for a long time and says — nothing. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s smile drops. From here it looks like {frenemy} thinks the rainbow is bad and is being too nice to say it out loud.
{friend}'s eyes prick. From here it looks like {frenemy} does not even care enough to talk about {friend}'s drawing. That feels like being invisible.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} doesn't like the drawing. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was {frenemy} actually doing while looking at the rainbow?
{frenemy} got stuck on the purple stripe. They were wondering HOW {friend} made the purple line so smooth. Their mouth was open to ask but their brain was still busy looking.
{frenemy} was trying to memorize every color in order so they could try the same rainbow at home later. They forgot that {friend} was waiting for words.
{friend} thought silence meant bad. {frenemy}'s silence actually meant 'wow, I am still looking.'
{friend} makes up a wiggly dance in the living room. While they spin, {friend} sees {frenemy} watching with a huge open-mouth grin. What does {friend} think the grin means?
{friend}'s feet stop. From here that grin looks like {frenemy} is laughing because the dance is embarrassing. {friend} wants to disappear.
{friend}'s cheeks burn. From here that grin looks like {frenemy} thinks the dance is goofy and a little bit weird.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} is laughing at them. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was making {frenemy} grin so wide?
{frenemy} thought the wiggly dance was the best thing they had seen all day. Their grin was the kind of grin you can't keep in. They were not laughing at all — they were happy-smiling.
{frenemy}'s grin was the 'I want to do that too' kind. Their feet were already bouncing along under the table. They were getting ready to ask if they could learn the dance.
{friend} thought the smile meant 'laughing at.' It actually meant 'I like this.' Same face, two readings.
{friend} builds a block tower as tall as their chin. They walk to the bathroom for one minute. They come back to find {frenemy} pushing the top block — CRASH. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s eyes get hot. From here it looks like {frenemy} chose the exact moment {friend} was away to knock the whole thing down.
{friend}'s hands clench. From here it looks like {frenemy} thought wrecking the tower would be funny without asking if {friend} would think so too.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} broke it on purpose. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What did {frenemy} think the tower was for?

choices.label: a game waiting to be played

steps.text: {frenemy} thought every block tower ends with one big satisfying CRASH — that was the WHOLE fun. They thought {friend} had walked away because they were ready for the crash part.
{frenemy} thought the tower was the START of a game — like a fort to attack with paper airplanes. They thought pushing the top block was step one of playing together.
{friend} saw their building wrecked. {frenemy} thought they were starting the next game. No bad guys here.

templates.title: The favorite song nobody sang along to

steps.text: {friend} plays their favorite song out loud. They look over at {frenemy} expecting big singing — but {frenemy} sits very quietly with a small face. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s smile fades. From here {frenemy}'s quiet face looks like 'this song is bad and I am suffering through it.'
{friend}'s cheeks go warm. From here {frenemy}'s quiet face looks like 'this song is for little kids and I am too old for it.'
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} does not like the song. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was happening inside {frenemy} during that song?
{frenemy} was singing every single word — but only inside their head. Out loud they were too quiet to hear, just lips barely moving. They actually love this song.
{frenemy} closed up because they wanted to hear every note carefully. They had a small face because all their feelings were busy listening. Loving a song doesn't always look big.
{friend} thought quiet meant 'bad song.' {frenemy}'s quiet actually meant 'I love it so much.'
{friend} has been sitting in the blue chair at the craft table all morning. They run to grab more paper. When they come back, {frenemy} is sitting in the blue chair. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s ears turn warm. From here it looks like {frenemy} watched {friend} leave and grabbed the chair right away. That feels unfair.
{friend}'s arms feel tight. From here it looks like {frenemy} had been waiting for the chair the whole time, watching for any chance.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} took their spot. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What did {frenemy} see when they walked up to the blue chair?
{frenemy} saw the blue chair with NOTHING in front of it — no paper, no markers, no in-progress drawing. They thought {friend} had cleaned up and finished. An empty desk meant an empty chair.
{frenemy} did not even know {friend} had been there. From their angle the chair just looked open, the way any empty chair looks open. They sat down without a second thought.
{friend} thought {frenemy} stole the spot. {frenemy} thought the spot was free. The chair did not tell either of them the whole story.
{friend} announces a great idea: a pillow fort with a SECRET door. Five minutes later, {frenemy} starts building a pillow fort with a SECRET door of their own. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s shoulders pull up. From here it looks like {frenemy} grabbed {friend}'s idea and pretended it was theirs. That feels like a copy without credit.
{friend}'s jaw goes tight. From here it looks like {frenemy} could not come up with anything of their own and had to use {friend}'s idea.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} copied them. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was going through {frenemy}'s head when they heard the secret-door idea?

choices.label: this is the best idea ever

steps.text: {frenemy} thought the secret-door idea was the coolest thing they had ever heard. To {frenemy}, building one too was a way of saying 'this idea is AMAZING' — not stealing it.
{frenemy} pictured two pillow forts side by side with two secret doors that could MEET in the middle. They were trying to expand {friend}'s idea into a bigger game for both of them.
{friend} thought {frenemy} was stealing. {frenemy} thought they were saying 'yes!' to a great idea.
{friend} leaves all their markers spread out on the floor. They come back from snack and every single marker is GONE. {frenemy} is the only one in the room. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s hands open and close. From here it looks like {frenemy} stashed the markers somewhere to tease {friend} or play a trick.
{friend}'s stomach knots. From here it looks like {frenemy} carried the markers off because they wanted them for themself.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} did something with the markers. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What did {frenemy} decide to do when they saw the markers everywhere?
{frenemy} saw the dog wandering over and worried the markers would get chewed. They grabbed every marker fast and put them all back in the marker bin to keep them safe.
{frenemy} heard a grown-up say 'clean-up time in five minutes' and wanted to help {friend} not get in trouble. They scooped the markers into the bin before {friend} got back.
{friend} thought the markers were taken. {frenemy} thought they were being a helper. The bin had them the whole time.
{friend} and {frenemy} are taking turns putting puzzle pieces in. It is {frenemy}'s turn and they have been staring at one piece for what feels like FOREVER. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s feet bounce. From here it looks like {frenemy} is taking forever ON PURPOSE because they like making {friend} wait.
{friend}'s hands fidget. From here it looks like {frenemy} is not even paying attention to the puzzle and is just zoning out.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} is wasting their turn. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was {frenemy} doing as they stared at that piece?
{frenemy} was matching the piece's edges to every open spot, one by one. They wanted to be sure it really fit before pushing it down — not slow on purpose, just careful.
{frenemy} could see the piece almost fit, but the picture on it was upside down. They were turning it in their head, trying to get it RIGHT so the puzzle would look perfect for both of them.
{friend} thought slow meant 'hogging.' {frenemy}'s slow meant 'making sure.' Both wanted to play.
{frenemy} cuts a piece of cake in two and hands the smaller half to {friend}. {friend} stares at their plate, then at {frenemy}'s bigger piece. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s mouth pulls down. From here it looks like {frenemy} did the cutting so THEY would get the bigger piece and {friend} would get the small one.
{friend}'s arms cross. From here it looks like {frenemy} just does not care about being fair, even with cake.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} took the bigger piece on purpose. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. Why did {frenemy} hand over the smaller piece?

choices.label: they thought it was the favor

steps.text: {frenemy} remembered that {friend} usually says they 'can't finish a whole big piece.' They thought giving {friend} the smaller half was being kind — less waste, less full belly.
Last time they shared, {friend} had picked the smaller piece on purpose. {frenemy} thought {friend} PREFERRED smaller, so they handed over the small half thinking it was {friend}'s favorite.
{friend} thought {frenemy} kept the big one. {frenemy} thought small was the kind gift. Cake looks different from each plate.

templates.title: The talking that filled the room

steps.text: {friend} is trying to read a book on the couch. {frenemy} is across the room talking at full volume about their new sticker pack — for a long, long, long time. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s ears pinch. From here it looks like {frenemy} is using a loud voice ON PURPOSE because they want to make {friend} stop reading.
{friend}'s shoulders rise. From here it looks like {frenemy} can see the book and just does not care that {friend} is trying to concentrate.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} is being too loud on purpose. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What did {frenemy} notice about how loud they were being?
{frenemy} was SO excited about the sticker pack their volume turned up without them even hearing it rise. Inside their head it felt like a normal voice the whole time.
{frenemy} could not see {friend}'s book from where they stood — only the back of the couch. They thought {friend} was just resting. A resting person doesn't need quiet.
{friend} thought 'loud on purpose.' {frenemy} thought 'normal voice, nobody reading.' Two ears, two stories.

templates.title: The swing that nobody got off

steps.text: There is only ONE swing left at the park. {frenemy} is on it. {friend} stands close by, waiting. The swinging goes on and on. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s feet stomp once. From here it looks like {frenemy} plans to stay on the swing until {friend} gives up and walks away.
{friend}'s chest hurts. From here it looks like {frenemy} hasn't even looked at {friend} once. {friend} feels invisible.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} is hogging the swing. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What rule about swings was {frenemy} using inside their head?
{frenemy} thought the rule was 'if someone wants a turn they have to come ASK.' They were waiting for {friend} to say 'my turn?' so they would know {friend} wanted on.
At {frenemy}'s house, swing turns were measured by counting to one hundred. They were almost done with their count. They thought it was a fair pattern, not a forever.
{friend} thought 'hogging.' {frenemy} thought 'waiting for the ask.' Different rules in two heads.

templates.title: The game that started without them

steps.text: {frenemy} starts a tag game with two other kids in the yard. {friend} is standing right by the gate. {frenemy} doesn't call them over. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s eyes get warm. From here it looks like {frenemy} picked the other kids and is leaving {friend} out on purpose.
{friend}'s feet feel heavy. From here it looks like {frenemy} is pretending not to see {friend} by the gate so they have an excuse.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} doesn't want them in the game. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was {frenemy} so focused on that they didn't call {friend}?
{frenemy} was busy teaching the two other kids a tricky new tag rule about safe-bases. Their whole brain was on getting the rule right. They were about to wave {friend} in once it stuck.
{frenemy} was deep in the 'how do we pick who is It' part. They had a counting rhyme going and could not stop in the middle. Inviting {friend} was the VERY next step, but the rhyme was first.
{friend} thought 'they don't want me.' {frenemy} was so focused on rules they hadn't gotten to the inviting part yet.
{friend} opens their lunchbox. There are tiny seaweed crackers their grandma made. {frenemy} stares at the crackers with a funny scrunched-up face. What does {friend} think the face means?
{friend}'s cheeks heat up. From here that scrunched face looks like 'ew, that lunch is so weird.' {friend} wants to close the box.
{friend}'s tummy tightens. From here that face looks like {frenemy} is silently making fun of {friend}'s grandma's cooking.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} thinks the lunch is gross. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was that scrunched face actually about?
{frenemy} had never seen seaweed crackers before. Their scrunched face was a 'wait, what ARE those, they look so cool' face. Inside their head they were already wondering if they tasted salty.
{frenemy} squinted at the crackers because the color reminded them of their cousin's favorite snack. They were quietly hoping {friend} might share a taste so they could try one too.
{friend} thought the face meant 'gross.' {frenemy}'s face meant 'curious.' New foods make funny faces.
{friend} sets their best dragon drawing on the couch. They turn around for a second. When they turn back, {frenemy} is sitting RIGHT on top of the drawing. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s breath catches. From here it looks like {frenemy} aimed for the drawing on purpose, to wreck it.
{friend}'s hands shake. From here it looks like {frenemy} just doesn't care what is on the couch before they plop down.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} squashed the dragon drawing. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What did {frenemy} see when they walked up to the couch?
The drawing was the same color as the cushion. From where {frenemy} stood, the couch looked totally empty. They sat down without ever seeing the paper was there.
{frenemy} was rushing to sit and start a new game with {friend}. Their eyes were on the toy bin, not the cushion. They never looked down before sitting.
{friend} thought the squashing was on purpose. {frenemy} never saw the dragon at all. Same couch, two views.

templates.title: The wave that wasn't waved back

steps.text: {friend} spots {frenemy} across the playground and waves BIG. {frenemy} walks right past, no wave back, no smile, like they aren't there at all. What does {friend} think?
{friend}'s arm drops. From here it looks like {frenemy} saw the wave perfectly and chose to ignore it on purpose.
{friend}'s chest pinches. From here it looks like {frenemy} does not want to be friends today and is walking past on purpose.
{friend} has decided: {frenemy} ignored the wave. That is how the scene looks from {friend}'s side.
Now let's see the same scene from {frenemy}'s side. What was happening for {frenemy} as they walked across the playground?
The sun was right in {frenemy}'s eyes. They could see almost nothing but bright squiggles. They walked past {friend} without seeing the wave, the smile, or {friend} themself.
{frenemy} was deep in a daydream about a story they wanted to tell {friend} later. Their eyes were looking forward but their brain was a million miles away. The wave never reached them.
{friend} thought 'ignored.' {frenemy} never saw the wave. Eyes can be open and still not looking.
{friend} and {frenemy} both reach for the last cookie on the plate at the same time. Four hands, one cookie. What should they try?
{friend} breaks the cookie carefully in half. Two small pieces instead of one big one — and now both kids have a snack.
How does {friend} feel? Sharing-proud. Their chest puffs up a little.
{frenemy} spots an apple in the fruit bowl across the counter. They grab it instead and leave the cookie for {friend}.
How does {frenemy} feel? Glad-they-thought-of-it, a little clever.
{friend} takes one small bite, then hands the rest to {frenemy}. Next snack-time the order flips.
How does {friend} feel? Patient. Waiting was easier than they expected.
{friend} hears that {frenemy} had a hard day at the park. They slide the cookie across the plate. {frenemy} says thank you.
How does {friend} feel? Generous, with a little wistful for the cookie they did not eat.
Two kids, one snack, no fight.
{friend} and {frenemy} both run to the one empty swing. Lunch is in five minutes, so there's only time for one good swing. What should they try?
{frenemy} sees the slide is wide open. They head over for a fast slide while {friend} takes the swing.
How does {frenemy} feel? Pretty good — the slide is fun too, and nobody had to wait.
{friend} counts twenty pumps, then hops off so {frenemy} can have twenty pumps before lunch.
How do both kids feel? Fair. Twenty is twenty, no matter who goes first.
{friend} notices {frenemy} only got to swing once yesterday. They wave {frenemy} onto the swing and step back.
How does {friend} feel? Generous. A little tired feet today, anyway.
Two kids, one swing, no tears.

templates.title: The last red marker in the box

steps.text: {friend} and {frenemy} are coloring side by side. They both reach for the red marker at the exact same second. What should they try?
{friend} uses the red marker on the top half of their picture, then slides it over so {frenemy} can use it on theirs.
How does {friend} feel? Sharing-proud. Their hand is a little tired from waiting but happy.
{frenemy} digs in the marker box and finds a pink marker. Close to red, and nobody else is using it.
How does {frenemy} feel? Clever. The pink looks even better on their drawing.
{friend} colors one shape red, then passes the marker to {frenemy}. They trade after every shape.
How do both kids feel? Patient. The drawings grow side by side.
{frenemy} is drawing a fire truck and really needs the red. {friend} hands the marker over and picks blue instead.
How does {friend} feel? Helpful. Their fire-truck could be a water-truck — also cool.
Two kids, one marker, two finished pictures.

templates.title: Both want to be the dog

steps.text: {friend} and {frenemy} are starting a pretend-play game. Both of them want to be the dog character. The dog only fits one kid. What should they try?
They invent two dogs — a big one and a puppy. {friend} plays the big dog, {frenemy} plays the puppy. The story has TWO dogs now.
How does {friend} feel? Inventive. Two dogs is more fun than one anyway.
{frenemy} decides to be the cat instead. The cat and the dog are best friends in this story.
How does {frenemy} feel? Glad-they-thought-of-it. Cat-noises are funny anyway.
{friend} is the dog for the first part of the story; halfway through, they switch and {frenemy} becomes the dog.
How do both kids feel? Patient. Half a turn each is fair.
{friend} remembers {frenemy} loves dogs more than anything. They wave {frenemy} into the dog role and pick the owner role instead.
How does {friend} feel? Generous, a little wistful, but the owner-role gets to give treats.
Two kids, one story, lots of barking.

templates.title: The front seat at the puppet show

steps.text: {friend} and {frenemy} both want the one front-row spot for the puppet show. There's a great view from there. What should they try?
{frenemy} grabs a chair and sets it up next to the front spot. Two front-row seats now. Problem solved.
How does {frenemy} feel? Clever. Their new seat is honestly just as good.
{friend} sits front-row for act one. At intermission they swap, and {frenemy} sits front-row for act two.
How do both kids feel? Fair. Each got the best view for half the show.
{friend} knows {frenemy} has never seen this puppet show before. They scoot back one row and wave {frenemy} into the front seat.
How does {friend} feel? Generous. Watching {frenemy} see it for the first time is its own kind of fun.
Two kids, one show, two happy puppet-watchers.
{friend} and {frenemy} both reach for the dollhouse elevator button. The elevator only has one button. What should they try?
They press the button TOGETHER, one finger each. The elevator goes up with both fingers on the button. Click.
How do both kids feel? Proud. Two fingers worked just as well as one.
{friend} pushes the button for the up trip. {frenemy} pushes it for the down trip. The elevator gets busy.
How does {friend} feel? Patient. Up and down is really two turns anyway.
{frenemy} got to push the button five times yesterday. They wave {friend} forward and say, 'your turn first today.'
How does {frenemy} feel? Grateful for yesterday, satisfied with waving today.
Two kids, one elevator, lots of trips up and down.
{friend} and {frenemy} both grab for the red toy car. It's the fastest one in the bin. What should they try?
They build a race track and race side by side. {friend} drives the red car on the inside lane, {frenemy} drives a blue car on the outside lane — and they switch cars halfway.
How do both kids feel? Proud. Racing together is better than racing alone.
{frenemy} digs and finds an orange car. Almost the same as red, and the wheels are even faster.
How does {frenemy} feel? Clever. The orange car turns out to be the cool one.
{friend} drives the red car around the track once. Then they hand it to {frenemy} for one lap. They keep trading.
How does {friend} feel? Patient. Watching a lap is almost as fun as driving one.
{friend} remembers they had the red car last time. They hand it over and pick the green car instead.
How does {friend} feel? Fair. Green cars are good too.
Two kids, one red car, a whole busy track.

templates.title: Who picks the next pretend-play role?

steps.text: {friend} and {frenemy} are about to start a new round of pretend-play. Both of them want to pick what kind of game it is. What should they try?
They invent a brand-new game that has TWO main characters — one each. Neither kid had to give up their idea.
How does {friend} feel? Inventive. Mixing two games made a better one.
{friend} picks today's game. {frenemy} picks tomorrow's. They write it on a sticky note so nobody forgets.
How does {frenemy} feel? Patient. Tomorrow is actually pretty close.
{frenemy} sees {friend} brought a new idea they really want to try. They say, 'you go ahead, I'll play along.'
How does {frenemy} feel? Generous. Playing along can be its own kind of fun.
Two kids, one game, both helped pick it.
{friend} and {frenemy} both flop onto the cozy corner of the rug. There's only room for one to really stretch out. What should they try?
They sit BACK TO BACK in the corner, each taking half the cozy spot. Both backs supported, both kids cozy.
How does {friend} feel? Pretty cozy, actually. Back-warmth is a bonus.
{frenemy} drags a pillow over and builds their own cozy spot near the wall. Just as soft, no fight.
How does {frenemy} feel? Clever. Pillow-corner might be even cozier.
{friend} stretches out in the corner for five minutes. Then they trade, and {frenemy} gets the spot for five minutes.
How do both kids feel? Patient. Five minutes feels long when you're cozy.
Two kids, one cozy corner, no shoving.

templates.title: Who feeds the stuffed cat first?

steps.text: {friend} and {frenemy} both want to give the stuffed cat its pretend breakfast. The cat only has one bowl. What should they try?
{friend} pours the pretend milk; {frenemy} adds the pretend kibble. Two helpers, one bowl, one very happy cat.
How do both kids feel? Proud. The cat purrs either way.
{friend} feeds the cat breakfast. At pretend lunchtime, {frenemy} gets to feed it.
How does {frenemy} feel? Patient. Lunch is only two pretend hours away.
{frenemy} fed the cat yesterday already. They wave {friend} forward with the bowl this time.
How does {frenemy} feel? Satisfied. The cat remembers who fed it yesterday.
Two kids, one cat, breakfast served.
{friend} and {frenemy} both want to be at the front of the pretend parade through the living room. Only one kid can lead. What should they try?
They invent a co-leader role: {friend} leads one side, {frenemy} leads the other side. The parade has two flag-bearers now.
How does {friend} feel? Inventive. Two leaders are more fun than one.
{friend} leads the first lap around the rug. {frenemy} leads the second lap. They keep trading.
How do both kids feel? Patient. Marching at the back is fun too — you get to see the leader.
{friend} led last time. They wave {frenemy} to the front of the line and pick up a drum to play from the back.
How does {friend} feel? Generous. Drums are loud and excellent.
Two kids, one parade, everyone marches.
{friend} and {frenemy} both spot the sparkly magic wand in the prop box. They both want it for their pretend spell-casting. What should they try?
They invent a TWO-WIZARD spell that needs both kids' hands on the wand to work. They cast together — pow.
How do both kids feel? Proud. Two-wizard magic is the strongest kind.
{frenemy} finds a long stick that looks just as wand-y. They wrap a star sticker on top — definitely a wand.
How does {frenemy} feel? Clever. Their stick-wand feels more theirs anyway.
{friend} casts three spells with the wand, then passes it to {frenemy} for three spells. They keep trading.
How does {friend} feel? Patient. Watching a spell is sometimes more fun than casting one.
{frenemy} has been planning their spell all morning. {friend} hands them the wand and says, 'cast yours first.'
How does {friend} feel? Generous. Watching the planned-spell happen is its own kind of magic.
Two kids, one wand, lots of pretend sparkles.

templates.title: Both want to be on the same team

steps.text: {friend} and {frenemy} are picking teams for a backyard game. Both kids want to be on the WINNING team — but every team needs the same number of players. What should they try?
They invent a new role: scorekeeper. The scorekeeper helps both teams and isn't on either. {frenemy} takes the role and {friend} joins the team that needed one more.
How does {frenemy} feel? Important. Scorekeepers see everything.
{friend} joins team A for round one. Round two, {friend} switches over to team B with {frenemy}. Everybody gets to be teammates eventually.
How do both kids feel? Patient. Two rounds is really two chances to win.
{frenemy} sees {friend} really wants to play with the kid on team B. They volunteer for team A instead and wave {friend} over to B.
How does {frenemy} feel? Generous, a little wistful, but cheering across the line counts too.
Two kids, two teams, one good game.
{friend} runs past the rug and accidentally knocks over {frenemy}'s tall block tower. The blocks scatter. {frenemy} looks scared, hands frozen. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry!" really fast and looks down. {frenemy} stays quiet — the apology was too quick to land.
{friend} tries again, slower: "I'm sorry for knocking over your tower. I was running too fast." {frenemy} nods.
{friend} kneels and starts re-stacking the blocks alone. It feels lonely. {frenemy}'s hands stay in their lap.
{friend} stops, looks up, and asks, "want to rebuild it together?" {frenemy} reaches for a block. They build side by side.
{friend} says, "don't be sad." {frenemy}'s shoulders stay tense — being told what to feel didn't help.
{friend} waits a beat and tries again: "you look scared. I'd be scared too if my tower fell." {frenemy}'s shoulders drop.
Repair takes more than one try. They keep playing.
{friend} started a tag game with two other kids and forgot to invite {frenemy}, who was right there. {frenemy} stands alone at the edge, watching. What does {friend} do?
{friend} jogs over: "sorry, sorry!" {frenemy} shrugs — a fast sorry doesn't tell them what {friend} is sorry FOR.
{friend} stops, breathes, and says, "I'm sorry I didn't invite you. I should have looked around first." {frenemy} nods.
{friend} says, "it's not a big deal." {frenemy} looks away — being told their feeling isn't a big deal made it bigger.
{friend} tries again: "you look left out. That would hurt me too." {frenemy} meets their eyes and steps closer.
{friend} waves: "come play later, okay?" {frenemy} doesn't move — "later" feels like another way of saying no-thanks.
{friend} walks over and says, "come play right now. You can be It first." {frenemy} grins and joins the game.
A late invite is still an invite. They run together.

templates.title: Said something mean about a drawing

steps.text: {friend} looked at {frenemy}'s drawing and said, "that doesn't look like a horse." {frenemy}'s face crumples and they cover the picture. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says "sorry, I didn't mean it!" but {frenemy} keeps the paper covered — "didn't mean it" doesn't undo the words.
{friend} tries again: "I'm sorry I said that about your drawing. It wasn't kind, and I'm the one who was wrong." {frenemy} uncovers the paper.
{friend} says, "don't cry." {frenemy}'s shoulders stay hunched — being told not to feel something made the feeling stick.
{friend} sits down next to them: "my words hurt your feelings. I'd be hurt too." {frenemy} wipes their eyes and nods.
{friend} says, "want to draw with me?" {frenemy} shakes their head — being asked to draw right after the comment feels like more pressure.
{friend} tries again: "want me to draw a horse next to yours? Yours can be the parent and mine the baby." {frenemy} slides the paper over.
The drawing now has two horses. They both color it in.
{frenemy} tripped on the rug and landed on their knees. {friend} laughed — just for a second, but {frenemy} heard it and turned red. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry, it just slipped out!" {frenemy} looks at the floor — blaming it on "slipping out" sounds like {friend} won't be careful next time.
{friend} kneels next to them: "I'm sorry I laughed when you fell. That wasn't kind. Are you okay?" {frenemy} nods, small.
{friend} says, "don't be embarrassed, it's nothing." {frenemy} stays curled up — being told their feeling is "nothing" made it heavier.
{friend} sits beside them: "you look embarrassed. I'd feel embarrassed too if someone laughed at me." {frenemy}'s shoulders soften.
{friend} reaches out a hand. {frenemy} doesn't take it — being helped up by the kid who laughed feels like the wrong order.
{friend} lowers the hand and waits. "I can sit with you until you're ready." After a moment, {frenemy} takes the hand.
They both stand up. The rug is the only thing still on the floor.

templates.title: Took the last marker without asking

steps.text: {friend} grabbed the last blue marker out of the box right as {frenemy} was reaching for it. {frenemy} pulls their hand back, eyes wide. What does {friend} do?
{friend} mutters, "sorry." {frenemy} stays quiet — a quick sorry while still holding the marker doesn't change anything.
{friend} sets the marker on the table: "I'm sorry I grabbed the marker. I saw you reaching." {frenemy} nods.
{friend} hands the marker over. {frenemy} takes it but their face is still tight — getting it now doesn't undo the grab.
{friend} digs through the box: "let me find you another color you like too." They pull out a teal. {frenemy} brightens.
{friend} offers a different marker — a yellow one. {frenemy} shakes their head; they wanted BLUE for the sky.
{friend} thinks, then offers a trade: "I'll use yellow on my picture. You take the blue." {frenemy} accepts.
One marker, two pictures. The sky is blue.
{friend} pressed too hard and snapped {frenemy}'s favorite purple crayon in half. {frenemy} stares at the two pieces. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry, it was old anyway." {frenemy} frowns — calling it "old" makes it sound like the loss doesn't count.
{friend} picks up both halves and tries again: "I'm sorry I broke your purple. I know it was your favorite." {frenemy}'s frown softens.
{friend} grabs the tape and tries to stick the two halves together. The tape doesn't hold; the crayon won't draw. {frenemy} sighs.
{friend} thinks again: "two halves means two crayons we can both use at once. Want to color the same picture together?" {frenemy} picks up one half and slides over.
{friend} offers their own purple crayon. {frenemy} looks at it but doesn't take it — a swap doesn't make the favorite one whole.
{friend} sits down: "you can keep this one, and I'll save my next purple for you too." {frenemy} accepts and tucks it into their tin.
Crayons can break. Friendships are bendier than that.
{frenemy} was in the middle of telling a long pretend-play story about dragons. {friend} cut in to add their own part and the whole thread got lost. {frenemy} stops talking. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry, keep going!" {frenemy} stays quiet — they don't remember where they were anymore.
{friend} tries again: "I'm sorry I cut in. Your story was good. Can you start that part over so I can hear it?" {frenemy} thinks, then begins again.
{friend} says, "don't be mad." {frenemy} crosses their arms — being told not to be mad made them feel madder.
{friend} tries again: "you look frustrated. I'd be frustrated too if someone cut me off in the middle." {frenemy} uncrosses their arms.
{friend} offers, "want me to be a quiet listener now?" {frenemy} hesitates — the offer sounds like {friend} wants this to be over fast.
{friend} sits down cross-legged: "I'll listen all the way to the end. The dragons can take as long as they need." {frenemy} smiles and continues.
The dragons get their full story. Both kids cheer at the end.
{frenemy} waved from across the room and {friend} walked right past, eyes on the floor. {frenemy}'s hand drops; they look down. {friend} realizes a minute later. What does {friend} do?
{friend} calls from across the room: "sorry, I didn't see you!" {frenemy} nods but stays sat down — a shout-apology from far away doesn't quite reach.
{friend} walks over and crouches: "I'm sorry I walked past. I was looking at the floor and didn't see your wave." {frenemy} smiles a little.
{friend} says, "it's no big deal!" {frenemy} shrugs — being told the moment was nothing made it feel like THEY were nothing.
{friend} tries again: "you look ignored. That would hurt me too." {frenemy} looks up and scoots over to make room.
{friend} waves big: "hi now! hi!" {frenemy} manages a small wave back — the loud catch-up wave feels a little late.
{friend} sits down next to them: "want to do something together? Just us, right now." {frenemy} nods and stands up.
One missed wave doesn't end a friendship. They walk off together.
{frenemy} showed off their rainbow-striped pants and polka-dot shirt. {friend} said, "that's weird." {frenemy}'s smile drops. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry, weird is good!" {frenemy} doesn't smile back — "weird is good" is still weird, and it sounds like a save.
{friend} tries again: "I'm sorry I said weird. That came out wrong. Your outfit is bright and I like the stripes." {frenemy}'s smile peeks back.
{friend} says, "don't be sad about your clothes!" {frenemy} looks down — being told not to be sad didn't make the sadness leave.
{friend} tries again: "you look hurt. If someone called my favorite outfit weird, I'd feel hurt too." {frenemy} looks up.
{friend} offers, "want to wear one of MY shirts?" {frenemy} shakes their head — they liked THIS outfit and the offer felt like a swap-out.
{friend} thinks again: "can I find something stripy of mine so we match?" {frenemy} brightens and nods.
They walk off, matching in stripes. Weird is good after all.

templates.title: Left a name off the team list

steps.text: {friend} wrote down the team list for the pretend game and forgot to put {frenemy} on it. {frenemy} reads the list twice, hoping. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "oh, sorry!" and keeps walking. {frenemy} stares at the paper — a sorry-on-the-go didn't change the list.
{friend} comes back and looks at the paper: "I'm sorry I left your name off. That was a real mistake, not just a typo." {frenemy} hands them the pen.
{friend} scribbles {frenemy}'s name in the margin in tiny letters. {frenemy} sees it — squeezed in the corner doesn't feel like being on the team.
{friend} flips the paper over and re-writes the WHOLE list with {frenemy}'s name first this time. {frenemy} smiles big.
{friend} offers, "you can be our cheerleader." {frenemy} shakes their head — they wanted to PLAY, not cheer.
{friend} thinks: "you can be on my team and we both go first." {frenemy} grins and joins the lineup.
A team list with everyone on it. The game begins.
{friend} walked across the rug and stepped right on {frenemy}'s last puzzle piece. It bent. {frenemy} holds up the crinkled piece. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry, didn't see it." {frenemy} doesn't look up — "didn't see" doesn't fix the bent piece.
{friend} kneels down: "I'm sorry I stepped on your piece. I should have looked where I was walking around your puzzle." {frenemy} hands them the piece.
{friend} tries to flatten the piece against the floor. It stays bent. {frenemy} sighs.
{friend} tries again: "let me press it under a book for a few minutes — that fixed mine once." They slide the piece under a board book together.
{friend} says, "don't be upset over a puzzle." {frenemy}'s shoulders go tight — telling them the feeling is too big made it bigger.
{friend} sits down: "you look frustrated. I'd be frustrated too if someone bent my last piece." {frenemy}'s shoulders loosen.
A bent piece still fits. The puzzle finishes.

templates.title: Skipped a turn in the game

steps.text: {friend} was so excited that they took two turns in a row and skipped {frenemy} completely. {frenemy} sets down their game piece. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry, my bad!" and rolls again. {frenemy} watches — saying sorry while taking ANOTHER turn doesn't count.
{friend} puts the dice down: "I'm sorry I skipped your turn. I got carried away. Your turn now — twice, to make it even." {frenemy} picks up the dice.
{friend} undoes their second move on the board. {frenemy} watches but their face stays still — undoing the move doesn't undo the missed turn.
{friend} also slides {frenemy}'s piece forward to where it would have been, and offers an extra roll: "your turn now, plus the one I missed." {frenemy} nods.
{friend} offers a swap: "you go first next round." {frenemy} doesn't reach for the dice — "next round" still feels like waiting.
{friend} thinks again: "actually, you go right now and I'll skip MY next turn to make it even." {frenemy} smiles and rolls.
The game keeps going. Both kids have the same number of turns now.

templates.title: Closed the book before they were done

steps.text: {friend} closed the picture book while {frenemy} was still looking at the last page. {frenemy}'s finger was still on the page. What does {friend} do?
{friend} says, "sorry, didn't know!" {frenemy} stays quiet — they had been pointing at the page for a while.
{friend} opens the book again: "I'm sorry I closed it. I should have checked if you were still looking." {frenemy} points back to the page.
{friend} flips back to the page and pushes the book toward {frenemy}. {frenemy} looks at it but their finger doesn't go back down — the moment got interrupted.
{friend} sits closer and asks, "what were you looking at? Show me." {frenemy} points to a tiny mouse in the corner of the picture. They study it together.
{friend} says, "don't be upset, it's just a book." {frenemy} pulls their hand into their lap — calling the book "just" made the feeling smaller and bigger at once.
{friend} tries again: "you look interrupted. I'd be frustrated too if someone closed my book." {frenemy} nods.
The book opens again. They find the mouse on every page now.

templates.title: Hid the teddy bear as a joke

steps.text: {friend} hid {frenemy}'s teddy bear behind the curtain as a joke. {frenemy} got scared and started looking everywhere. The joke didn't land. What does {friend} do?
{friend} pulls the teddy out: "haha, sorry, joke!" {frenemy} grabs the teddy and holds it tight — calling it a joke makes the scare feel less serious to {friend}, but not to {frenemy}.
{friend} sits down: "I'm sorry I hid Teddy. I thought it would be funny but it scared you. I won't hide your stuff again." {frenemy} hugs the bear and nods.
{friend} hands the teddy back. {frenemy} takes it but won't put it down — getting it back doesn't undo the lost-feeling.
{friend} thinks: "let's make Teddy a safe spot where I PROMISE I won't move them." They build a small pillow nest together.
{friend} says, "it was just a joke!" {frenemy} squeezes the teddy harder — "just a joke" makes their scared-feeling feel wrong.
{friend} tries again: "you got really scared. I'd be scared too if my favorite went missing." {frenemy} nods, eyes wet but soft.
Teddy gets a nest. Jokes work better when both kids laugh.

templates.title: Kept playing the song that was too loud

steps.text: {frenemy} said the song was too loud for their ears, but {friend} kept playing it. {frenemy} covered their ears and turned away. What does {friend} do?
{friend} pauses the music: "sorry, I forgot." {frenemy} keeps their hands near their ears — "forgot" sounds like it could happen again.
{friend} tries again: "I'm sorry I kept playing it after you said it was too loud. I should have listened the first time." {frenemy} lowers their hands.
{friend} says, "don't be upset, it's a fun song." {frenemy} stays turned away — being told the song is fun doesn't change that it was too loud.
{friend} tries again: "your ears were overwhelmed. Loud sounds hurt mine too sometimes." {frenemy} turns back.
{friend} offers, "I'll skip this song." {frenemy} shrugs — skipping it sounds like the song just won today, not like {friend} understood.
{friend} thinks again: "you pick the volume. I'll wait until you say it's good." {frenemy} turns the dial down themselves and presses play.
A quieter song works for both of them. They dance, ears happy.
